1: When Ephraim spoke — the verse opens with a participial construction recalling a past era when Ephraim held authority. The Hebrew nasa' (lifted up, trembled) gives the sense of nations shuddering at Ephraim's word. The contrast with the following line is sharp: greatness led to apostasy. asham ba-ba'al — he incurred guilt through Baal. Asham (guilt, be guilty) is the root of the guilt-offering (asham); using it here frames Israel's apostasy as a cultic debt that must be satisfied.
2: They keep sinning — yasafu laḥeto, a Qal perfect with consecutive force; the sins compound. masukit (molten images, cast metal) vs. pesilim (carved idols) — both terms appear; comprehensive coverage of the idol industry. The phrase zovḥei adam 'agalim yishaqun is cryptic: most read 'those who sacrifice men [or: to men?] kiss calves.' Nashaq (kiss) was a cultic act of veneration (1 Kgs 19:18; Ps 2:12). The calf-kiss completes the apostasy tableau from v1.
3: Morning mist / early dew — two paired similes for impermanence: anan-boqer (morning cloud) and tal mashkim holekh (dew that goes early). The same pairing appears in 6:4 to describe Israel's covenant loyalty (ḥesed). Here the poet turns the accusation back: as you said of your love, so now I say of you. Two more images follow: motz (chaff) driven from threshing floor and 'ashan (smoke) from a window — all emphasize rapid, total disappearance. Four images of vanishing in one verse: the rhetoric of divine inevitability.
4: I am the LORD your God from Egypt — the covenant formula in its exodus form, echoing Exod 20:2. me'erets mitsrayim (from the land of Egypt) anchors identity in redemptive history. The exclusivity claim follows: elohim zulati lo teda' — you shall know no god except me. The key term is moshia' (savior, deliverer) in the final clause: u-moshi'a 'ayin bilti — there is no savior except/apart from me. Moshia' shares the root y-sh-' with Joshua (Yehoshua) and Jesus (Yeshua); the noun denotes active, effective rescue. The claim is absolute: no foreign patron-deity holds this role.
5: I knew you in the wilderness — da'ati (I knew) with the covenant sense of yada': relational, elective knowledge (cf. Amos 3:2 'you only have I known'). be'erets talmaveth — in a land of deep drought (or deadly drought; tsalmaveth lit. 'shadow of death'). The wilderness period was YHWH's provision against all natural odds; knowing Israel there was an act of committed care.
6: When I fed them, they were satisfied — kira'otam vayisbe'u: satisfaction led to pride. The verb sequence tracks the covenant-curse dynamic: satiation (saba') → pride (rum lev, heart lifted) → forgetting (shakehu, they forgot me). This is the Deuteronomic warning fulfilled (Deut 8:11-14): beware lest you eat and are satisfied and your heart is lifted up and you forget the LORD. Hosea treats the wilderness as the formative period of covenant fidelity; sedentary abundance broke it.
7: I will be like a lion to them — YHWH shifts from shepherd/lover to predator. Three animals: keshav (lion), namer (leopard), and the bear of v8. The lion imagery is common for divine wrath (Amos 1:2; Isa 31:4); the leopard (namer) is notable for speed and stealth — al derekh asher (on the road to Assyria), the exile route itself becomes the ambush site. The reversal of Exodus: instead of leading them out of the wilderness, YHWH meets them like a predator on the return road.
8: Like a bear robbed of her cubs — dov shakkul (a bereaved bear) was proverbially ferocious (2 Sam 17:8; Prov 17:12). va'eqra' seghor libam — I will tear open the enclosure of their heart (or: the covering over their heart). Seghor can mean enclosure, cage, or the chest cavity. The graphic imagery continues: ve'okhalem sham ka-laviv — I will devour them there like a lion. The accumulation of predator images (lion → leopard → bear → lion again) is a rhetorical intensification: no escape from divine judgment.
9: You are destroyed, Israel — shiḥetekhah Yisra'el: the second-person address is stark. The rest of the verse is a crux: ki vi be'ezrekhah. Most read: 'for you are against me [YHWH], your only help.' The implied contrast is total: the one being who could help is now the one bringing ruin. The RSV/ESV read 'your help is in me alone' as the implied positive; the context of judgment makes the negative reading ('who is against you') equally possible. The MKT renders the destructive irony: your only rescuer is now the source of destruction.
10: Where is your king now — the taunt formula ehi (where is…?) mocks the institutions Israel trusted. malkekha (your king) and shopetekha (your judges) are called into question: she-yoshi'akha bekhol arekhah — who might save you in all your cities. Yoshi'akha is the Hiphil of y-sh-' again — the political leaders offered the same salvation that only YHWH can give. The irony: they demanded a king (1 Sam 8); now that king is exposed as helpless. va'omar (and I said) — divine permission is behind the kingship that now fails them.
11: I gave you a king in my anger — Eten lekha melekh be'api — the Saul tradition (1 Sam 8-12) stands in the background; the divine concession to monarchy is recast as anger. va'eqqaḥ be'evrati (I took away in my wrath) — both giving and taking are expressions of divine judgment. The king is simultaneously gift and curse; the monarchy contains within it the seeds of its own termination. This verse anchors the political theology: human kingship is inherently unstable because it displaced YHWH's direct rule.
12: Ephraim's iniquity is bound up — tsarur 'avon Ephrayim: the guilt is stored, preserved, not forgotten. tsarur (bound, tied up) suggests a sealed bundle kept for later reckoning (cf. Job 14:17 'my transgression is sealed up in a bag'). tsefunah ḥatato — his sin is stored/hidden. The two verbs form a hendiadys of preserved judgment: nothing has been discharged, all is held in reserve. The image anticipates the birth imagery of v13: all this stored guilt will now force its way out.
13: Birth pangs come upon him — ḥevlei yoledah yavo'u lo: labor contractions arrive. But then the devastating twist: hu' ben lo' ḥakham ki-'et lo' ya'amod — he is a son without wisdom, for at the proper time he does not present himself at the mouth of the womb. Misbar banim — the mouth/opening of the womb. The child refuses to be born at the crisis moment. The image of the reluctant child-in-birth is a metaphor for Israel refusing the turn (shuv) that the pangs of judgment are designed to produce. Birth pangs that could lead to new life instead lead to suffocation if the child will not come.
14: Shall I ransom them from Sheol — miyyad she'ol efdemem and mimmavet ag'alem: the key crux. The Hebrew verbs are Qal imperfects: efdeh (I will ransom / shall I ransom?) and eg'al (I will redeem / shall I redeem?). In context (vv1-13 building toward judgment), the MT points toward rhetorical questions with a negative answer: 'Compassion is hidden from my eyes' (niḥam yissater me'einai) is the clincher — a refusal of relenting. However, Paul's citation in 1 Cor 15:55 ('Where, O death, is your sting? Where, O Sheol, is your victory?') uses the LXX version, which renders these as boasts/taunts directed at death rather than divine questions. The LXX pou (where?) turns the Hebrew interrogatives into triumphant challenges. Dever (plague) and qotev (pestilence) are addressed as death's weapons. The ambiguity is textually significant: the same words that in the MT context convey divine withdrawal become, in Paul's eschatological reading, the language of resurrection victory. Both readings arise legitimately from the consonantal text.
15: Though he flourishes among his brothers — ben yafri' ben ahim: the wordplay on Ephraim (from parah, to be fruitful). Even in fruitfulness, doom comes: yavo' qadim ruaḥ YHWH — the east wind (sirocco) of YHWH comes. Ruaḥ qadim (east wind) is the desert wind from the wilderness — the same direction as Assyria. It will dry up the spring (maqor) and the fountain (ma'yan). The spring imagery inverts the dew of 14:5 (coming chapter): before the restoration is the devastating drought. Hu' yishsasseh otsar kol-keli ḥemdah — it will plunder the treasury of every precious vessel. Military conquest follows agricultural ruin.
16: Samaria shall bear her guilt — te'sham Shomron: the capital city as metonym for the entire northern kingdom. ki maretah be'eloheiha — because she has rebelled against her God. The judgment is graphic: be-ḥerev yippolu (they will fall by the sword), ollelihem yeruttashu (their infants dashed), and harariotav yebukqa'u (their pregnant women ripped open). These are standard ANE warfare atrocities, also invoked as covenant curses (2 Kgs 8:12; Amos 1:13). The verse closes out ch13 at maximum intensity before ch14's abrupt pivot to restoration invitation.
